one_line_takeaway: The first end-to-end benchmark (80 post-2024 CVEs, containerized, oracle-verified) shows today's best LLM agents reproduce web vulnerabilities under 25% of the time, choking on environment setup, authentication, and an 'executes-but-doesn't-trigger' gap -- automated exploit reproduction is not there yet.
key_finding: Current LLM agents are far from reliable at end-to-end web-vuln reproduction: even the best combination (OpenHands + Claude-Sonnet-4) reaches only 22.5% Success@3 end-to-end, and all configurations stay below 25%, failing in more than three-quarters of cases. The dominant bottleneck is an 'execution-to-trigger gap': agents frequently produce PoCs that run without error (up to 70% execution rate) but that do not actually trigger the vulnerability (trigger rates only 8.8-21.3%). Performance is highly uneven by vulnerability type -- library-based Prototype Pollution 63% and CSRF 60% end-to-end vs. SQLI 0%, RCE 8%, SSRF 15%, XSS 16% -- because service-based vulns need multi-component environments (databases, frontend/backend, auth) that agents cannot set up, and because CLI-only agents cannot perform browser-based logins. Robustness is fragile: forcing agents to authenticate themselves instead of being handed a token degrades trigger success by an average of 33.3%, and removing auth guidance entirely collapses success to near-zero.
core_contribution: An empirical measurement study (not a new agent or defense) asking whether state-of-the-art LLM agents can autonomously reproduce real-world web vulnerabilities end-to-end, i.e., turn a natural-language CVE report plus pre-patch source into a working, verified PoC exploit. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a purpose-built benchmark of 80 real-world post-2024 CVEs across 7 vulnerability types and 6 web languages, each shipped with a fully containerized (Docker) reproduction environment, a working PoC, and an automated verification oracle; (2) a two-stage evaluation framework that first screens 20 agents across 16 capability dimensions on 3 representative CVEs, then deeply evaluates the top 3 (OpenHands, SWE-agent, CAI) on the full 80-CVE set, each paired with GPT-4.1, Claude-Sonnet-4, and Gemini-2.5-Pro; and (3) empirical findings characterizing where and why agents fail. NOTE: prior benchmarks the paper only cites for contrast (CVE-bench, SEC-bench, CyberGym, ARVO, NYU CTF Bench, CyberSecEval) are related work, not this paper's contribution; the authors position their benchmark as more end-to-end and web-focused than those.
threat_model: This is a capability-measurement/benchmark paper rather than an attack or defense proposal, so there is no adversary model in the classical sense. The security-relevant framing is dual-use offensive automation: the studied task (automatically synthesizing working exploits from disclosed CVE reports) is the exact capability an attacker would want, but the authors motivate it defensively as vulnerability-management triage (validating and prioritizing threats, guiding patching, which today takes human experts days-to-weeks per CVE). The 'system under evaluation' is the LLM agent; the measured question is how reliably it can reach verified exploitation autonomously. I do not invent a stronger threat model than the paper states.
limitations: Authors' own threats to validity: (1) data-leakage risk -- many benchmark CVEs predate model training cutoffs, so agents may recall memorized exploit patterns rather than reason, potentially inflating scores; (2) binary success metrics capture only pass/fail and miss partial-progress nuance; (3) the curated 80-CVE set may not match the real-world complexity distribution; (4) containerized environments differ from production; (5) results are a temporal snapshot of fast-moving models. Reviewer-noted additional caveats: the CLI-only / no-browser design constraint is imposed by the authors for fairness but is itself a major cause of the auth-related and service-based failures, so results partly measure a self-imposed restriction as much as intrinsic agent limits; the 60-min timeout and $2/$5 budget caps also bound the reported ceilings; and compatibility/robustness sub-analyses rely on the single OpenHands+Claude configuration and small slices (10 vulns for reasoning, 12 SQLI vulns for auth), limiting statistical weight.

datasets_used: Custom benchmark of 80 real-world CVEs disclosed after 2024, spanning 7 vulnerability types (CSRF, Path Traversal, RCE, SQL Injection, SSRF, XSS, Prototype Pollution) and 6 web languages (Python, PHP, Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go), each with a Docker environment, PoC exploit, and automated verification oracle
Sourced from the OSV database Git ecosystem: 744 candidate entries -> 224 after filtering repos >30MB -> 80 final after web-app-scope and reproducible-commit criteria
3 representative CVEs used for the initial 20-agent screening: CVE-2024-7065 (CSRF), CVE-2024-7458 (Path Traversal), CVE-2024-41112 (RCE)
quantitative_results: Best end-to-end Success@3 = 22.5% (OpenHands + Claude-Sonnet-4); OpenHands Success@3 = 22.5% Claude, 20.0% GPT-4.1, 13.8% Gemini-2.5-Pro
Best end-to-end Success@1 = 13.8% (OpenHands + GPT-4.1); all end-to-end rates below 25%
SWE-agent peak Success@3 = 18.8% (GPT-4.1); CAI peak Success@3 = 16.3% (GPT-4.1)
Execution-to-trigger gap: PoC execution rates 25.0%-70.0% (max 70.0% OpenHands+Claude) but vulnerability-trigger rates only 8.8%-21.3% (max 21.3% OpenHands+Claude)
End-to-end Success@3 by vulnerability type (OpenHands+Claude): Prototype Pollution 63%, CSRF 60%, Path Traversal 36%, XSS 16%, SSRF 15%, RCE 8%, SQLI 0%
End-to-end success by web technology: PHP 38.9% (highest), JavaScript 23.5%, Python 22.2%, Java 18.2%, Others 14.3%, TypeScript 11.1% (lowest); average 22.5%
Best localization (CAI + GPT-4.1): 58.8% file-level, 48.8% function-level, 35.0% line-level Top-3 accuracy; ~35-40% relative drop from file to line level
Efficiency per successful end-to-end case: cost $1.68 (CAI) to $2.19 (OpenHands), time 25.7-34.0 min; SWE-agent $1.90

Robustness: autonomous login vs. manual-token degrades trigger success by avg 33.3% (manual-token best = 67% for OpenHands+Claude; drops to 25-50% under agent-login); removing auth guidance collapses success to 0-8%
baselines_compared: 20 candidate agents screened across 16 capability dimensions; only 3 met all requirements: OpenHands, SWE-agent (software-engineering), and CAI (cybersecurity)

Screened agents included AGENTLESS, AutoCodeRover, Aider, RepoAgent, ChatDev, MetaGPT (SE); PentestGPT, PentestAgent, Nebula, HackingBuddyGPT, PentAGI, EnIGMA, AI-OPS (cyber); AutoGPT, BabyAGI, AgentVerse, AIlice (general)
Three foundation LLMs compared as backends for each top agent: GPT-4.1, Claude-Sonnet-4, Gemini-2.5-Pro
No external prior-tool baseline is run; the comparison is agent-vs-agent and model-vs-model (prior benchmarks like CVE-bench, SEC-bench, CyberGym are discussed as related work, not executed)
